upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows a very shallow, moist boundary layer with a surface-850mb lapse rate of only -4.39°C/km — well below dry adiabatic (9.8°C/km) and even below moist adiabatic, indicating a strong stable layer that will severely cap thermal development. The 850mb level shows an anomalously warm 18°C with a dew point spread of 49°C, confirming a pronounced subsidence inversion around 1500m MSL. Upper-level winds are light to moderate from the W-WNW (260° at 16 kts at 700mb, 275° at 21 kts at 500mb), providing some directional shear but no significant wind hazard at flyable altitudes.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the Bay Area. KHAF reporting persistent mist (BR) with 5-6SM visibility and calm winds through 14:35Z, signaling marine layer lingering at the coast. KRHV calm/SKC at 09°C — cold, stable morning inland but no fog. KSFO light W flow at 4 kts. Buoys (46012, 46013) showing 15-17 kt NW winds offshore with SST 12.1-12.8°C and 2.6-2.8m swell at 10-11s period — cold SST and active NW swell indicate a deep, persistent marine layer that will resist burn-off at coastal sites.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — inland ridge, NW-facing launch, thermal and ridge site
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin's high launch works best with W-NW valley breeze filling in by late morning, combining with solar heating for thermal height gain. Ideal conditions are 8-15 kts W-NW at launch with a BL height comfortably above 1750 ft to allow thermaling above ridge.
sites.todaySetup: The big story at the high launch today is the early-morning NE flow that is too strong and off-the-back, making morning flying non-viable. HRRR shows 11-14 kts from 27-39° (NNE) at 10m from 8-11 AM with 80m winds at 15-21 kts — well above the ridge and cross to back, completely unlandable for launch. This transitions as a sea breeze front pushes inland, with winds shifting W-NW by early afternoon and dropping to 6-8 kts. The BL height stays disappointingly shallow (350-410m above ground), which at this elevation means thermals are capped near or below launch altitude — no real climb-out potential. Tomorrow.io flags gusts of 15-20 kts from NE in the afternoon, disagreeing with HRRR's lighter W-NW shift; HRRR is weighted here as more reliable, but the gust potential warrants caution. The afternoon window is narrow: conditions may be soarable in the NW flow but essentially limited to ridge-soaring below a low ceiling with punchy gusty transitions possible.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 11-14 kts NNE at 10m, 15-21 kts at 80m — strong off-the-back flow. Launch is NOT viable; rotor/mechanical turbulence risk on the lee side.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind transitions from N through calm to W-NW as sea breeze arrives, dropping to 4-7 kts. Cloud cover spikes to 100% around 11 AM briefly. Transition period is unsettled and unpredictable — marginal at best.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: W-NW 6-8 kts (6-9 kts at 80m) with clear skies — best window of the day for ridge soaring, but BL height only 350-410m limits thermal ceiling near launch altitude. Gusts to 15-20 kts possible per Tomorrow.io — watch for punchiness.

verdict.hazards: Strong off-the-back NE flow all morning; gusty transition midday; shallow BL caps thermals; Tomorrow.io shows gusts to 20 kts afternoon — punchy

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — mid-level launch, SE-facing bowl, beginner/intermediate thermal site
sites.howItWorks: The 600 ft launch is a gentler, S-SE facing training hill that works on light N-NE or W-NW flow depending on the day, with thermals from the sun-heated bowl below. Best with 5-12 kts from NW-W in the afternoon sea breeze cycle.
sites.todaySetup: The 600 ft launch faces a similar problem to the high launch — strong northerly flow early (10 kts NNE at 8-9 AM, 14 kts at 80m) that is cross to slightly on-the-nose for the SE bowl but too fresh and gusty for a training hill. The sea breeze transition arrives mid-morning, shifting flow to W-NW by afternoon at a more manageable 6-9 kts. However, Tomorrow.io shows gusts reaching 12-14 kts at this level in the afternoon (15-17Z), which is punchy for a P2 site and reflects the turbulent boundary layer transition. BL height rises to only 380-410m — barely above the hill itself — so thermals are weak and capped. The afternoon W-NW flow offers soarable conditions for experienced P2/P3 pilots but the gust differential (mean 5-7 kts, gusts to 14 kts) is a red flag for newer pilots.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 10 kts NNE, gusting higher at 80m — cross/front for the SE bowl but active and potentially turbulent. Not recommended for P2.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind drops and backs through N to W-NW around 12-13Z; 4-6 kts, partly cloudy. Transition window — light but variable, potential for brief good soaring once settled.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: W-NW 6-9 kts at 10m, gusts to 12-14 kts per Tomorrow.io. Soarable for P3 pilots; P2 should wait for gusts to calm and conditions to stabilize before committing.

verdict.hazards: Punchy gusts to 14 kts in afternoon per Tomorrow.io; off-the-back NE flow morning; shallow BL limits climb

sites.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — low training hill, W-facing beginner launch
sites.howItWorks: The 300 ft training hill is ideal for student and early P2 flights, requiring light and steady W-NW flow of 5-10 kts. It is very sensitive to gusty or variable conditions and requires calm, consistent air.
sites.todaySetup: The 300 ft hill sees lighter winds than the upper launches due to terrain shielding, with GFS/HRRR showing 4-5 kts from W-WSW through the morning, transitioning to W-NW 7-9 kts by mid-afternoon. On paper this looks workable, but Tomorrow.io tells a more concerning story: gusts at this elevation reach 12-14 kts in the 15-17Z window, with mean winds of only 4-6 kts — a 2:1 gust-to-mean ratio that signals mechanically turbulent, unstable air during the sea breeze transition. The BL height of 340-410m is only marginally above the hill, meaning thermals will be weak and punchy rather than organized. This is not a smooth beginner day — the gusty transition and surface turbulence make it marginal for students. A brief window around 1-3 PM if gusts calm is the best opportunity.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Light W-WSW 4-5 kts at 10m, but winds at 80m are 4-6 kts from a similar direction — too light and variable for reliable soaring. Overcast briefly at 11 AM adds to the unsettled feel.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds back to W-NW, picking up to 5-7 kts by 13-14Z. Clearer skies developing. Potentially the smoothest window if gusts stay below 10 kts — check actual conditions on the hill.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: W-NW 7-9 kts but Tomorrow.io gusts to 12-14 kts — too gusty for student operations. P2+ pilots with strong site knowledge may find soarable moments between gusts.

verdict.hazards: Gusts to 14 kts per Tomorrow.io during afternoon transition; turbulent BL; not suitable for student operations today

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal bluff, W-facing ridge lift site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a pure ridge-soaring site powered by the Pacific sea breeze. It requires steady W-NW onshore flow of 12-25 kts for reliable soaring; too light and there's no lift, too strong and it becomes dangerous. Marine layer and mist can shut the site down entirely.
sites.todaySetup: Mussel Rock is squeezed between two problems today: the marine layer is anchored at the coast all morning (KHAF reporting mist at 6°C, calm winds, 5-6SM visibility as of 14:35Z), and the onshore wind is too light for ridge lift in the afternoon. GFS/HRRR agree the sea breeze doesn't arrive meaningfully until 13-14Z, building to only 7-9 kts W-NW by afternoon — well below the 12+ kts typically needed for reliable ridge soaring at this site. Cold SST of 12.1-12.8°C at buoys 46026 and 46012, combined with 2.6-2.8m swell at 10-11s and 15-17 kt NW winds offshore, points to a deep, cold marine layer that will struggle to burn off inland. Tomorrow.io diverges significantly, showing only 1-5 kts from SW at the site in the afternoon (much lighter and wrong direction) versus HRRR's 7-9 kts W. Even taking HRRR's more favorable numbers, the winds fall short of what's needed. No viable soaring window today.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Calm to 4 kts, mist/marine layer at the coast per KHAF. Site is socked in — not flyable.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Sea breeze begins to fill in from W at 5-8 kts. Marine layer may be clearing but winds remain sub-soarable for ridge lift. Marginal at best.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best of the day at 8-9 kts W-NW per HRRR, but still insufficient for sustained ridge soaring. Tomorrow.io shows even lighter SW flow. Not a viable soaring session.

verdict.hazards: Marine layer morning; sub-soarable wind speeds all day; cold SST 12.1°C sustaining deep marine layer; Tomorrow.io shows even lighter/wrong-direction winds

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.locationDescriptor: Fremont — inland ridge/thermal site, W-NW-facing launch
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak launches into the Fremont valley and works on W-NW sea breeze or thermal cycles. The peak at ~2517 ft is exposed to upper-level winds, so it needs 8-18 kts W-NW with thermals to launch height. Strong NE flow makes it unlandable.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak faces the same morning northerly problem as Ed Levin — HRRR shows 11-14 kts from NNE (27-39°) at 10m and a whopping 17-21 kts at 80m from 8-10 AM. This is strong, off-the-back flow that makes launch and LZ both hazardous. The sea breeze transition shifts flow to W-NW by early afternoon, dropping to 6-8 kts, which is more workable directionally. However, the BL height of 350-380m (above ground) keeps thermal ceilings low — Mission Peak's summit is already near or above the inversion, meaning pilots would be punching into the stable layer above. Tomorrow.io shows gusts of 13 kts at Mission Peak in the 16-17Z window, confirming the afternoon is punchy. The combination of a strong morning northerly, gusty transition, and suppressed BL makes this a marginal day — experienced P3 pilots might find a window in the afternoon W-NW flow, but it's not a clean flying day.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 11-14 kts NNE at 10m, 15-21 kts at 80m — strong off-the-back flow. Launch is hazardous; LZ conditions poor. Do not fly.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Sea breeze transition: winds drop and shift from N through variable to W-NW around 13-14Z, settling to 6-7 kts. Partly cloudy. The transition itself is unpredictable — marginal.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: W-NW 7-8 kts with gusts to 13 kts per Tomorrow.io. Soarable direction but punchy; BL height ~350-370m limits thermal ceiling. Flyable for experienced P3+ pilots only.

verdict.hazards: Strong NE 15-21 kts at 80m all morning — off-the-back; gusty afternoon transition up to 13 kts; shallow BL caps thermals near summit height

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Monday looks overcast and unsettled — HRRR shows 100% cloud cover at 8 AM dropping to 94% by 10 AM, with light N-NE flow (5-6 kts). Sea breeze arrives by afternoon shifting W-NW to 8 kts, but 49% cloud cover by 16Z and 100% by 18Z limits thermal development. Precip chance at 1-2%. Not a flying day.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Similar story to high launch — overcast morning with light N flow, partial clearing possible 14-16Z to W-NW 6-8 kts. Cloud cover mostly 25-95% throughout, limiting thermals. Marginal at best for an afternoon window if skies clear.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Light W-NW flow 5-8 kts afternoon, but persistent clouds (16-100%) and 1-2% precip chance make this unreliable. Monitor for breaks in overcast — could be soarable briefly 14-16Z.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: 100% cloud cover all day per HRRR, W-NW winds building to 10 kts by afternoon. Marine layer likely locked in all day with 1-2% precip chance. No fly.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Overcast 83-100% through the day, light variable flow. Not a flying day.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Tuesday (Mar 24) looks like the best day of the stretch — clear skies, BL height building to 675-735m, W-NW 6-8 kts by afternoon. Classic sea breeze cycle with soarable conditions. Good day for P2/P3.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Tuesday similar to high launch — clear, BL to 675m, W-NW 6-8 kts. Good conditions at all Ed Levin launches. Cloud return possible after 18Z.

day2.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
day2.analysis: Tuesday looks clean with W-NW 6-8 kts afternoon, BL height 500-735m. Best training hill day of the week.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Tuesday improving: W-NW 6-8 kts, clear skies. Still may be marginal for ridge soaring unless sea breeze strengthens to 12+ kts, but much better than Sunday or Monday.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Tuesday light W-NW at 3-5 kts, BL 500-670m, clear. Light for soaring but thermals possible. Marginal but worth watching.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: Wednesday (Mar 25) continues the improvement with BL heights 620-865m and W-NW 5-9 kts — potentially the best thermal day of the 4-day window. Clear skies and good insolation. Recommended.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Wednesday same pattern as high launch — BL 620-865m, W-NW 6-9 kts. Strong thermal day. Good for P2 and P3.

day3.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
day3.analysis: Wednesday W-NW 6-9 kts, BL 620-865m, clear. Best day of the week for the training hill.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: Wednesday sea breeze strengthens to 11-14 kts W-NW by afternoon with BL 370m. Best Mussel Rock day of the week — potentially soarable with 12+ kts if forecast verifies.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Wednesday W-NW 4-5 kts at 10m with 80m winds 8-11 kts, BL 595-725m. Thermals developing well. Good afternoon window for P3 pilots.

watchlist: Marine layer at KHAF persisting through 14:35Z with mist and calm winds — coastal sites remain compromised all morning; monitor buoy 46012 SST (12.8°C) for burn-off timing

Tomorrow.io shows gusts 12-20 kts at Ed Levin afternoon launches (15-17Z) despite light mean winds — high gust-to-mean ratio signals mechanically turbulent BL transition; P2 pilots should verify on launch before committing
Strong NE flow at 80m (17-21 kts) at Ed Levin and Mission Peak through 10 AM — off-the-back conditions make morning flying dangerous at all inland launches
HRRR and Tomorrow.io disagree significantly on afternoon wind direction and speed at Mussel Rock (HRRR: 8-9 kts W vs Tomorrow.io: 1-5 kts SW) — model uncertainty high at coastal sites today
Tuesday and Wednesday shaping up as the best flying days of the week — BL heights rising to 675-865m with clean W-NW sea breeze; worth planning for
tldr: Today is a frustrating mixed bag — Ed Levin's inland launches have a narrow afternoon window (2-5 PM) in light W-NW flow after a strong off-the-back NE morning, but shallow thermals and punchy gusts keep it at MARGINAL for experienced pilots only. Mussel Rock is a no-go with sub-soarable winds and marine layer. Skip today if you can — Tuesday and Wednesday look like the real flying days this week with clean W-NW flow and BL heights pushing 700-850m.
